subject: No water at all on the 3100 block of Washington and the street is flooding
body_clean: Mayor Okonkwo, I am writing at 6:40 this morning because something is very wrong on our block. I live at 3142 Washington Blvd, just east of Village Hall, and around 5:15 AM the water in my house dropped to nothing. We turned the kitchen tap on to make coffee and got a sputter of air and a little brown sludge, then nothing at all. My neighbor two doors down says the same thing happened to her around the same time. When I went outside to check, there is water coming up through the pavement in the middle of Washington Blvd, basically right in front of 3130, and it is already starting to freeze into a sheet of ice across both lanes. It is about 9 degrees out here. The water is bubbling up steadily, not just a trickle, and it has spread maybe forty feet down the gutter line on the south side. I am worried somebody is going to wreck their car on that ice or that a kid walking to the Metra station slips on it. I called the non-emergency police line and they said they would notify Public Works but I have not seen a truck yet. Can someone please tell me what is going on, when we will have water back, and whether the water that did come out of my tap is safe? I have a six-month-old in the house and I do not want to be mixing formula with bad water. Please advise as soon as you can. This block pays its water bills on time and I would appreciate a straight answer.

subject: Confirmed water main break Washington Blvd at 3130 - crew dispatched
body_clean: Mayor, Mr. Whitlock, copying the relevant staff so everyone is working from the same facts. We have confirmed a main break on Washington Blvd in front of 3130, roughly 200 feet east of Village Hall at 3200. This is on the 8-inch cast iron main that runs the length of Washington; per our atlas this segment was installed in 1958 and it is the same line that gave us trouble back in February 2023. My first crew (DiMeo, plus two operators and the backhoe) was on scene at 6:55 AM. Here is what we know so far. Static pressure in the district has dropped from our normal 62 PSI to about 18 PSI at the Washington/25th hydrant, which is consistent with a substantial break, not a service line. We have isolated the break by closing two valves, the one at Washington and Marshall and the one at Washington and Geneva. That takes pressure off the broken section but it also means everyone between those two valves is out of water until we cut in the repair. That is approximately 140 service addresses, the 3000 through 3300 blocks of Washington plus the short legs of Marshall and Geneva that feed off it. To Mr. Whitlock's safety question directly: do not drink the water even after it comes back, and do not use it for formula, until further notice. Any time we lose positive pressure on a main like this we have to treat it as a potential contamination event and the County will require a precautionary boil order. I have already looped in our health officer on that. For the ice on the roadway, I am sending a second truck with salt and we will cone off the south lane. Excavation to expose the break will start within the hour once JULIE marks are confirmed clear. I will send a repair-time estimate by mid-morning.

subject: Precautionary boil order issued for Washington Blvd break zone
body_clean: Mayor and team, per the loss of pressure Frank described, I am issuing a precautionary boil order for the affected zone effective immediately, 8:10 AM today. This follows IEPA guidance and our standing protocol whenever distribution pressure falls below 20 PSI; we measured 18 PSI at Washington/25th, so we are squarely in mandatory-notice territory. The boil order covers every service address between the Marshall Ave and Geneva Ave valves, which Frank counts at roughly 140 accounts across the 3000-3300 blocks of Washington Blvd and the feeder legs on Marshall and Geneva. The notice language is the standard one: residents in the zone should bring all water to a rolling boil for at least one minute (three minutes is what I tell people to be safe) before drinking, making ice, brushing teeth, washing produce, or preparing food or infant formula. Mr. Whitlock, that answers your question directly and I am sorry you are dealing with this with a newborn in the house. Bottled water is the safest option for the baby until the order lifts. Bellwood does not have a bottled-water cache, but I have asked the assistant to check whether the Jewel on Mannheim or the township can stage some pallets at Village Hall for pickup. The order stays in place until I have TWO consecutive sets of clear bacteriological samples taken at least 16 hours apart, which is the County lab's requirement, not just one. Realistically that means the earliest possible lift is the day after the repair is complete and the main is back to pressure and flushed. I will draft the door-hanger and a robocall script and get them to Tina for the reverse-911 within the hour. I will also notify the Cook County Department of Public Health since they require contemporaneous notice on any boil order over 100 connections.

subject: Brown water and now the boil order robocall - what about my water heater
body_clean: Mayor Okonkwo, I got the robocall about the boil order about twenty minutes ago and I have some questions nobody has answered yet. I am at 3225 Washington Blvd, right in the zone. Before the water cut out entirely around 5:30 this morning, what came out of my faucets was rust brown, almost like tea, and it left a gritty residue in the bottom of the bathtub I had started to fill. My concern is that I ran the washing machine on a hot cycle at about 5 AM before any of this happened, and now I am reading that sediment from a main break can clog up your water heater and your aerators and even ruin a dishwasher. Who is responsible if my appliances are damaged by the village's broken main? I did not do anything wrong here. The main is 67 years old according to what one of the workers outside told my husband, and frankly if the village has been running water through 1958 pipe this whole time then this is on the village, not on the residents. Second, I run a small in-home daycare licensed for six children and I cannot operate without safe water for handwashing and bottle prep. I have already had to call three families and tell them to keep their kids home today, which means I am losing income through no fault of my own. Is the village going to credit my water bill for the days I cannot use the water, and is there any kind of relief for a licensed home business that has to close because of this? I would like a real answer, not a form letter. I have lived on this block for nineteen years and I have never missed a payment.

subject: Repair status midday - main exposed, break confirmed, ETA tonight
body_clean: Mayor, update as of 12:30 PM. We have the main fully exposed. The break is a circumferential crack about 14 inches long on the bottom of the 8-inch cast iron, a classic cold-weather beam break where the frozen ground heaves and snaps the pipe at a weak point. Soil temperature and the fact that we have had three straight nights below 5 degrees is almost certainly the trigger. The hole is about nine feet down and we are fighting groundwater seeping into the excavation, so we have a trash pump running continuously. Plan is to cut out the failed section and install a stainless full-circle repair clamp; if the pipe wall is too far gone on either side of the clamp we will cut in a new spool with two sleeves instead. Parts are on the way from our stock at the Frederick Ave yard plus a backup clamp coming from the supplier in Melrose Park. Realistic timeline: clamp in place by 4 to 5 PM, then we slowly re-pressurize and check for leaks at the repair, then flush the mains through the hydrants at Washington/Marshall and Washington/Geneva to clear sediment and rust before we restore service. I expect water back to most of the 140 affected addresses between 8 and 10 PM tonight, barring complications. To be very clear for Priya and the billing side: restoring water does NOT lift the boil order. Even after pressure is back, the order holds until her two clear samples come back. Tom, I would like you in the loop on whether this 1958 segment goes on the capital replacement list; this is the second failure on this exact run since 2023 and I do not think it is the last. Resurfacing the trench patch on Washington will have to wait for warmer weather; we will do a cold-mix temporary patch tonight.

subject: Billing credit plan for the 140 affected accounts plus Ms. Aurelio question
body_clean: Mayor, David, copying Priya and Frank. Now that we have a count of affected accounts I want to get ahead of the billing piece so residents are not left wondering. Here is what I propose, pending David's sign-off on the finance side. First, I have pulled the account list for every service address between the Marshall and Geneva valves. It comes to 138 active accounts, two are vacant. For each of those, I propose we waive the full water and sewer volume charges for the entire affected billing cycle, not just the outage hours, because flushing rust and running taps to clear sediment will inflate everyone's metered usage and it would be unfair to bill them for water they had to dump. Our rate is currently $6.85 per 1,000 gallons water plus $4.10 sewer; a typical residential account uses about 4,000 gallons a month, so a full-cycle volume waiver runs roughly $44 per account, call it about $6,000 total across all 138. The fixed availability charge I would leave on, since that covers the infrastructure that we are in fact repairing. Second, for the boil-order days specifically I propose a flat $25 service-disruption credit per occupied account on top of the volume waiver, which is consistent with what we did after the 2023 break; that is another roughly $3,400. Total exposure is in the ballpark of $9,400, which I think is defensible and modest. On Ms. Aurelio's appliance-damage question at 3225, that is a liability claim, not a billing matter, and she should be directed to file with the village's risk pool through the clerk's office rather than through utility billing. I can apply credits automatically; residents will not need to request them. David, can you confirm the credits can come out of the water fund operating line and do not need board action under the threshold?

subject: Sign-off on water credits with two conditions
body_clean: Lorena, Mayor, this works and I can approve it administratively. Walking through the numbers: the combined volume waiver plus the $25 disruption credit lands around $9,400, which is well under the $25,000 threshold in our purchasing and adjustment policy that would require Village Board action, so we do not need to put this on the agenda. It comes out of the Water/Sewer Fund operating budget, specifically the rate-stabilization contingency line we carry exactly for events like this; we have about $40,000 left in that line for FY25 and this will not strain it. Two conditions before you run the credits. One, hold the actual posting until Frank confirms the main is repaired and Priya confirms the boil order is lifted, so we credit against the correct closed billing cycle and do not have to reverse anything if the timeline slips into the next read. Two, I want a clean one-page list of the 138 accounts with the credit amount per account so we have an audit trail; our outside auditors flagged the 2023 break adjustments because there was no supporting schedule, and I do not want a repeat finding. On Ms. Aurelio's appliance and lost-daycare-income claim, Lorena is right that this is not a billing question. Route it to risk management. I would caution the mayor's office against promising her anything on damages in writing; the village carries coverage through the intergovernmental risk pool and any claim goes through their adjuster, who will assess whether a 1958 main reaching end of life constitutes negligence or ordinary infrastructure failure. Those are very different liability outcomes and it is not our call to make. Separately, Tom and I should talk about whether the recurring failures on this run justify pulling the Washington Blvd replacement forward in the capital plan, possibly against the IEPA low-interest loan program.

subject: Update to residents - Washington Blvd water main repair and boil order
body_clean: To our neighbors on Washington Blvd and the surrounding blocks, I want to give you a straight, plain-language update on where things stand as of this evening. Early this morning a water main dating to 1958 cracked under Washington Blvd just east of Village Hall. Our Public Works crews, led by Director DiMeo, were on the scene within an hour and have worked in single-digit temperatures all day in a nine-foot-deep excavation to fix it. The broken section has been exposed and the repair is going in now. We expect water service to be restored to the roughly 140 affected homes and the home businesses on this block between 8 and 10 PM tonight. I want to be honest with you about two things. First, a boil order remains in effect for the affected zone and it will stay in effect even after your water comes back. Please boil all water for drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, washing produce, and especially for infant formula, for at least one minute, until we notify you the order is lifted. Our health officer cannot lift it until we have two consecutive clean water samples taken sixteen hours apart, which the County lab requires; the earliest that can happen is the day after the repair is finished. We are working to stage bottled water at Village Hall, 3200 Washington Blvd, for families who need it tonight, especially those with infants or medical needs. Second, you will not be charged for water you could not use or had to flush. The village is automatically waiving water and sewer volume charges for this billing cycle for every affected account, and applying an additional disruption credit. You do not need to call or fill out anything; the credit will appear on your next bill. If you believe the broken main damaged a water heater or appliance, please contact the Village Clerk's office to file a claim. I am grateful for your patience. Crews will be out there until this is done right.

subject: Water restored - pressure back to normal, mains flushed
body_clean: Mayor, good news to close out the night. The repair held. We ended up cutting in a new 18-inch spool of ductile iron with two stainless sleeves rather than relying on the clamp alone, because the pipe wall on the east side of the break was badly graphitized and pitting under the hammer test; a clamp would have been a band-aid that failed again by next winter. That added about two hours but it is the right fix. We slowly brought the section back up and re-pressurized starting at 7:40 PM. Static pressure across the district is back to 61 to 62 PSI as of 9:15 PM, right where it should be, and we held it for 30 minutes with no pressure decay at the repair, which tells me the joints are tight and the line is not leaking. We then flushed through the hydrants at Washington/Marshall and Washington/Geneva for about 25 minutes each until the discharge ran clear; residents may still see some rusty water at first draw tonight and should run their cold tap a few minutes before use, which is normal after any flush. Water service is restored to all 138 occupied accounts as of about 9:00 PM. The trench is backfilled and we put down a cold-mix temporary patch over the cut; it will need a permanent restoration once asphalt plants reopen in spring, and I have flagged it for the spring patch list. One reminder for everyone copied: service is back but the boil order is NOT lifted. That is Priya's call and depends on her samples. Tom, I will get you the failure photos and the pipe condition notes for the capital file; this is exactly the kind of documentation we want if we are going after IEPA loan money for the Washington replacement.

subject: Boil order LIFTED - both sample sets clear
body_clean: Mayor and team, I am very glad to report the boil order is officially lifted as of 2:15 PM today. Here is the sample chain so it is documented. After Frank restored pressure and flushed the mains the night of the break, my first set of bacteriological samples was collected the following morning at 8:30 AM from three points: a hydrant at Washington/Marshall, an interior tap at 3142 Washington (Mr. Whitlock kindly let us sample), and a tap at 3225 Washington (Ms. Aurelio's daycare). All three came back absent for total coliform and E. coli from the County lab. Per protocol I could not lift on one clean set; I needed a second set taken at least 16 hours later. That second set was collected at 1:00 AM today from the same three locations and also came back absent across the board. Two consecutive clean sets, more than 16 hours apart, satisfies both the IEPA requirement and Cook County DPH, so the order is lifted. Tina is pushing the all-clear robocall and door hangers now; the message tells residents they may resume normal water use but should flush their cold lines for a few minutes first and consider replacing any in-line faucet filters or fridge filters that ran during the rusty period. I have notified Cook County DPH of the lift to close out their file. From a public health standpoint this is now closed. Lorena, this means you are clear to post the billing credits against this cycle per David's condition. Mr. Whitlock and Ms. Aurelio, thank you both for your patience and for letting us into your homes to sample; that genuinely sped up the all-clear.

subject: Thank you and one follow-up on the credit and my claim
body_clean: Mayor Okonkwo, I want to say thank you. I was pretty hot when I wrote you yesterday morning and I stand by my questions, but I have to give credit where it is due. The crews were out there in brutal cold all day and night, the robocalls actually kept us informed, and somebody from the health department came and sampled right at my kitchen sink, which made me feel like a real person and not a case number. We got the all-clear call this afternoon and my tap water is running clear again. I reopened the daycare this morning once I saw the first samples were clean. Two quick follow-ups so I can close the book on this. First, I see you are crediting the water and sewer charges automatically, which I appreciate; can you confirm that will show on my February bill so I know to look for it? I do not want to assume and then be surprised. Second, I did go ahead and call the Clerk's office about my water heater, which has been making a knocking noise since the sediment went through it and the bottom is full of grit. They took my information and said an adjuster from the risk pool would follow up. I just want to make sure that is actually in motion and not going to fall through the cracks now that the emergency is over. I am not looking to make trouble, I just want my heater looked at and a fair answer. Either way, please pass my thanks along to Director DiMeo's crew and to the woman who came to take the samples. Nineteen years on this block and that is the most responsive I have ever seen the village be. Thank you.
